chunks.title: Catalog Description
chunks.content: Design and Analysis of Industrial Systems with Simulation. (2-3). Credit 3. In-depth study into the design-modeling and subsequent analysis of contemporary production/service systems; factory/service systems are modeled using the ARENA/SIMAN V simulation-animation language; emphasis is placed on the critical analysis of alternative flow designs of modeled systems using flow and economic parameters to assess system improvement. Prerequisites: Grade of C or better in ISEN 210 and ISEN 355.

chunks.title: Additional Course Details
chunks.content:
There is an approved change to the catalog description to reflect the move away from the ARENA/SIMAN V simulation-automation language. The revised catalog description is stated below:
In-depth study into the design-modeling and subsequent analysis of contemporary production/service systems; factory/service systems are modeled using a modern simulation software; emphasis is placed on the critical analysis of alternative flow designs of modeled systems using flow and economic parameters to assess system improvement.
We will be using the Simio simulation software in this course as a continuation of the software used in the prerequisite course ISEN 355.

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content: Prerequisite/Corequisite(s): Grade of C or better in ISEN 210 and ISEN 355.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:
• develop and analyze complex models for industrial engineering problems using commercially available discrete event simulation software,
• interpret simulation output using valid statistical methods and make appropriate recommendations, and
• analyze data to determine appropriate input distributions using valid statistical methods.

chunks.title: Special Course Designation
chunks.content: None

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
This material Is: Required
Simio and Simulation - Modeling, Analysis, Applications - 7th Edition
Authors: Jeffrey S. Smith and David T. Sturrock
Publication Date: August 2024
Edition: 7th Edition
URL for Resource: https://textbook.simio.com/SASMAA7/
Notes:
The book is available online at the Simio website.

This material Is: Recommended
Simulation Modeling with SIMIO: A Workbook
Authors: Jeffrey A. Joines and Michael E. Kuhl
Publication Date: 2025
Edition: 6th Edition
URL for Resource: https://textbook.simio.com/Workbook6/
Notes:
An excellent resource for learning more about building complex Simio models.

This material Is: Optional
Discrete-Event Simulation
Authors: Jerry Banks, John S. Carson II, Barry L. Nelson, and David M. Nicol
Publisher: Pearson-Prentice Hall
Publication Date: 2010
Edition: Fifth Edition
Notes:
This is a good reference text

This material Is: Optional
Simulation Modeling and Analysis
Authors: Averill M. Law
Publisher: McGraw Hill
Publication Date: 2024
Edition: 6th Edition
Notes:
This is a good reference text for learning more about simulation theory.

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content: Use your engineering/math skills to compute your score based on the weights described in the Grade Components and Weights section above.

chunks.title: Grade Components and Weights
chunks.content:
There are three exams in the course. There will be weekly lab assignments, some graded homework, regular quizzes, and a project.
• Exam 1 and Exam 2 are each worth 10%. The lab exam is worth 30%.
• The lab assignments together will be worth 15%. Submissions will not be accepted after the deadline with the exception of a university excused absence.
• There will be regular quizzes during the semester. The weight of each quiz is dependent on the number of questions in the quiz. All the quizzes together will be worth 10%. Quizzes must be taken in the classroom during the designated quiz time.
• Attempting a quiz outside of the classroom is not permitted and will be treated as a violation of the Aggie Honor Code.
• Some of the homework will be collected and graded. The homework grade is worth 5%.
• The project will be worth 20%. More details about the project will be provided during the semester.

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
Grades assigned are: A for 90% - 100%, B for 80% - 89.9%, C for 70% - 79.9%, D for 60% - 69.9%, F for below 60%. Any possibility of a curve on grades will be announced in the last few days of the semester.
Any disagreements regarding a grade received on any graded material must be discussed within two business days of the return of the graded material. No grade will be changed beyond the two business days limit.
Grades will be maintained in the gradebook within Canvas. Please note that the automated total and grade calculations on the Canvas gradebook are useless, and you should not rely on them.

chunks.title: Note About Homework
chunks.content: There will be homework assignments that will be assigned regularly. Some of the homework will be collected and graded. It is in your interest to work on the homework problems and develop the solutions. The solutions will be posted for you to compare your work. You can expect to see quiz questions and exam problems based on the homework problems.

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content: Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

chunks.title: Course Specific Late Work Policy
chunks.content: Late work is not accepted in this course.

chunks.title: Course Schedule
chunks.content:
Weekly schedule of topics
| Week | Topic |
|---|---|
| Week 1 1/12/26 | Review of discrete event simulation; system and model; Analysis of Production System: Push/Pull/CONWIP for single machine and flowline |
| Week 2 1/19/26 | No class meeting on Monday, January 19 [MLK Day]
Analysis of Production System: Push/Pull/CONWIP for single machine and flowline |
| Week 3 1/26/26 | Exploring the use of sequences, basic nodes, transporters in a production system |
| Week 4 2/2/26 | Handling queues; balking and reneging |
| Week 5 2/9/26 | Introduction to simulation-based optimization; use of OptQuest; handling financial costs |
| Week 6 2/16/26 | Supply chain and inventory control using simulation |
| Week 7 2/23/26 | First Exam Monday, February 23
Supply chain and inventory control continued; ranking and selection of scenarios; KN algorithm; using OptQuest to optimize parameters; multi-objective functions; specifying constraints |
| Week 8 3/2/26 | Exploring Tables, Relational Tables, Task Sequences, Material Consumption and Production, Resources and Workers, Setup Times and Material Replenishment |
| Week of 3/9/26 | Spring Break – no classes |
| Week 9 3/16/26 | Lab Exam Monday, March 16
Continuation of Tables, Relational Tables, Task Sequences, Material Consumption and Production, Resources and Workers, Setup Times and Material Replenishment |
| Week 10 3/23/26 | Exploring scheduling using Simio |
| Week 11 3/30/26 | Sub models: creating submodels for reuse |
| Week 12 4/6/26 | No classes on April 4 and 6 due to instructor travel (to be confirmed) |
| Week 13 4/13/26 | Sub classing: exploring subclassing of Simio objects to customize requirements |
| Week 14 4/20/26 | Second Exam Monday, April 20
Subclassing continued; Project Presentation and Report on April 22 |
| Week 15 4/27/26 | Wrap up/summary of course on April 27. |
NOTE: Some topics may be changed based on the pace of the class.

chunks.title: Additional Course Information
chunks.content:
Copyright Notice
All documents used in this course are copyrighted. Here, "documents" means all materials generated for this class, including, but not limited to, syllabi, quizzes, exams, problem sets, slide sets, and all materials appearing on CANVAS or sent to you via email. Because these materials are copyrighted, you do not have the right to copy any of them for any purpose other than your own personal academic use unless I expressly grant permission. In particular, course materials may not be given or sold to any non-profit or for-profit enterprise.

chunks.title: Software
chunks.content:
The Industrial and Systems Engineering Department uses Simio under a grant from Simio LLC (https://www.simio.com). Although Simio is installed in the ISEN Cloud, I strongly recommend that you install a copy of the software on your PC. You will find the detailed instructions on Canvas and the Technology Support portion of this syllabus. You will likely find this advantageous for working at home and in the classroom.
Simio does not work with the Mac Operating System (MacOS), but some students with Macs indicated that Simio worked well using Bootcamp, but I have no personal experience with using Simio on a Mac. Simio has provided some helpful resources for Mac users. This can be found at https://www.simio.com/publications/simio-for-mac/.
Simio is available in the Engineering Desktop available through the Aggie Virtual Desktop – see https://service.tamu.edu/TDClient/36/Portal/KB/ArticleDet?ID=657 for more information.

chunks.title: Expectations
chunks.content:
• Students are expected to attend all class and lab meetings.
• Students should come prepared to the class and lab meetings whenever there are any assigned reading or review assignments.
• Students are expected to bring their own computer for every class and lab meeting.
• Missed work due to unexcused absence will not be accepted.

chunks.title: Class Communication
chunks.content:
Communication and announcements with the class will be maintained using email and Canvas.
• Most of the class communication will be via email through the TAMUDirect mailing list maintained by the university. Students are expected to carefully read all the emails in their entirety.
• Some of the important announcements will also be posted in the “Announcements” section of Canvas.

chunks.title: AI Statement
chunks.content:
According to the Texas A&M University Definitions of Academic Misconduct, plagiarism is the appropriation of another person's ideas, processes, results or words without giving appropriate credit (aggiehonor.tamu.edu). You should credit your use of anyone else's words, graphic images, or ideas using standard citation styles. Artificial Intelligence (AI) text generators and natural language processing tools (colloquially, chatbots - such as ChatGPT), audio, computer code, video, and image generators should not be used for any work for this class without explicit permission of the instructor and appropriate attribution. This includes, but is not limited to,
i. Creating or revising drafts
ii. Editing your work
iii. Reviewing a peer's work
This excludes pre-existing software additions such as spelling and grammar checkers, which are acceptable.

chunks.title: Simio License for your personal computer
chunks.content:
To request a Student License, visit https://forms.simio.com/f/student-license and fill out the form to receive a Simio Student License. Your Student License Claim Code is 00150-9s0xrnBf5q. To be approved automatically, make sure to use the claim code and your university email address. If the code is omitted or incorrect, your request will require manual intervention, which may take up to five (5) business days. Note, we cannot provide technical support to students. Instead, please talk to your instructor; if they are unable to help resolve your problem, they can send your concern to Simio for support.
You can find important information about licenses, Simio version compatibility, installation, and troubleshooting at https://www.simio.com/academic-licensing/troubleshooting/
We will be using Simio Version 19.280 during Spring 2026.

chunks.title: Learning Resources
chunks.content:
Some resources that will be helpful in the process of learning Simio.
• Learning resources available from Simio on their website.
• Simio has a YouTube channel with a number of featured videos.
• A series of 12 Simio Tutorials by Professor Jeff Smith at Auburn University.
• A YouTube channel maintained by Professor Jeff Smith at Auburn University explaining simulation and Simio concepts with examples.

chunks.title: Course Specific Attendance Policy
chunks.content:
Students are expected to attend class in person and to complete all assignments. Lab assignment and homework submissions and attempting a quiz after the posted deadline will not be accepted unless there is a university excused absence.
Please note that the rule regarding job interviews is as follows: “Mandatory interviews for permanent, full-time employment or full-time internships (including those that are part of a cooperative education program) that have a duration of at least 10 weeks, provided that such interviews are related to the student's academic program and provided that the interviews are fixed date by employer policy and cannot be rescheduled. A student may not request excused absences for employment or internship interviews for more than one scheduled class meeting in one academic term.” Also, “Documentation from the employer must be provided 5 business days in advance."

chunks.title: Course Specific Makeup Work Policy
chunks.content:
Students are expected to attend class in person and to complete all assignments. Lab assignment and homework submissions and attempting a quiz after the posted deadline will not be accepted unless there is a university excused absence.
Make-up exam will be only for students that have provided documentation supporting their absence in accordance with the excused absence policy described in Student Rule 7.
